questions.question: Why are log systems often more expensive to keep fully searchable than metrics systems?
options: Because logs usually have larger, less-structured payloads and more flexible query needs.
Because metrics always require full-text indexes on every value.
Because logs cannot be archived.
Because log agents never batch events.
questions.explanation: Logs are heavier and less structured than metrics.

questions.question: What is a strong reason to place a durable bus between collectors and indexers?
options: It decouples ingest from indexing so short indexing slowdowns do not drop all logs.
It guarantees every search query is free.
It removes the need for redaction.
It makes archive retention unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Decoupling helps absorb spikes and failures.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals a parser rollout problem?
options: Parser failure rate and schema drift after deploy.
Average laptop battery level of developers.
Median HTTP title length in logs.
The number of dashboards pinned by users.
questions.explanation: Parser breakage shows up in parse-failure and drift signals.

questions.question: Why is early redaction preferable to indexing raw logs everywhere first?
options: It reduces the privacy blast radius and downstream cleanup burden.
It guarantees every log becomes searchable faster.
It removes the need for retention policies.
It prevents any parser bugs from occurring.
questions.explanation: Redact early to avoid spreading sensitive data.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with differentiated backpressure policies for log classes?
options: You protect critical signals better but add policy complexity and fairness debates.
You eliminate all need for archives.
You guarantee uniform user experience for every tenant.
You make replay impossible.
questions.explanation: Priority policies help, but they are harder to operate.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly addressed by replay support?
options: A parser or enrichment bug that needs old events reprocessed.
A user forgetting a dashboard filter.
A browser rendering issue in the search UI.
A cold archive becoming cheaper.
questions.explanation: Replay helps recover from processing mistakes.

questions.question: Why should hot searchable retention usually stay shorter than cold archive retention?
options: Because indexed search is expensive, while older data can often move to cheaper storage.
Because old logs are never useful.
Because archives cannot be queried at all.
Because search only works on today's data.
questions.explanation: Hot search is valuable but costly.

questions.question: What is the better platform response when one tenant overwhelms shared indexing capacity?
options: Apply quotas or deferred indexing for that tenant rather than letting everyone degrade equally.
Delete the global archive immediately.
Turn off redaction for faster ingest.
Drop all logs from every tenant.
